political.s: <cite index="5-1">JPMorgan Chase PAC raised $1,196,493 in the 2023-2024 election cycle</cite>, with verified FEC data showing right-leaning donations (55% to Republicans). <cite index="1-7">Contributions are directed on a bi-partisan basis</cite>, though the PAC maintains a rightward tilt. <cite index="4-1">The bank paused political donations following January 6 but resumed giving while avoiding Republicans who voted to overturn the 2020 election</cite>.

charity.s: <cite index="12-1">JPMorgan Chase gives over $220 million annually through the JPMorgan Chase Foundation to not-for-profit entities in more than 40 countries</cite>. <cite index="12-2,12-3">In November 2023, the company made commitments worth $3.5 million toward apprenticeship programs to help underserved communities attain quality work experience</cite>. <cite index="20-1">The foundation supports programs designed to promote workforce readiness, small business expansion, financial capability, and community development</cite>.
charity.focus: Workforce readiness, small business, financial capability, community development, affordable housing

environment.s: <cite index="21-1,21-7">JPMorgan Chase has backed away from its pledge to cut the carbon footprint of its corporate offices by 40 percent by 2030</cite>. <cite index="23-6">By the end of 2024, JPMorgan had achieved only a 14% reduction in operational emissions compared with its 2017 baseline</cite>. <cite index="21-9,21-10">The bank remains committed to investing $1 trillion to support renewable energy and climate initiatives by 2030, with $309 billion deployed so far</cite>.

labor.s: <cite index="32-1">JPMorgan Chase emerged as a leader with programs focused on fair and equitable compensation, employee career development, and inclusive hiring practices</cite>. However, <cite index="34-1,34-3">in January 2025 the bank mandated all 317,233 employees return to the office five days a week, with employees reporting tensions over insufficient desks and poor workplace conditions</cite>. <cite index="37-1">The U.S. Department of Labor sued JPMorgan for systematically discriminating against female employees in compensation</cite>.

dei.s: <cite index="42-1,42-3">JPMorgan Chase Chief Operating Officer replaced the word 'equity' with 'opportunity,' renaming the program to diversity, opportunity and inclusion (DOI)</cite>. <cite index="41-7">In its most recent annual report, JPMorgan changed a section previously titled 'diversity, equity and inclusion' to 'workforce composition'</cite>. <cite index="43-6,43-7">The company joined others in removing or toning down DEI language, with CEO Dimon stating the bank would scale back certain DEI initiatives viewed as wasteful while maintaining outreach to diverse communities</cite>.

animals.s: N/A - Banking institution with no animal testing or products

guns.s: N/A - Financial services company; does not manufacture or directly sell firearms

privacy.s: <cite index="56-1">JPMorgan Chase has a structured approach towards privacy, focusing on how personal information is collected, used, shared, and protected</cite>. <cite index="51-2,51-3">The company shares personal information among JPMorgan Chase family members and third parties outside customers' home countries, which may have different data protection requirements</cite>. <cite index="56-4,56-5">The practices ensure compliance with federal laws and provide avenues for customer control, maintaining a balance between operational necessities and customer privacy</cite>.

execPay.s: <cite index="62-3">CEO James Dimon received total compensation of $43.0 million in 2025</cite>. <cite index="66-10">Roughly 88% of the CEO's variable pay is deferred into performance share units</cite>. <cite index="63-3">The compensation structure utilizes clawbacks, strict share retention requirements, and an absence of golden parachutes to enforce accountability</cite>.

fec.committeeName: JPMORGAN CHASE & CO. FEDERAL POLITICAL ACTION COMMITTEE

sec.note: Pay ratio extracted from proxy filing — verify manually for accuracy
